chunks.text: Japan is one of the most earthquake - prone nations in the world.
It lies in a region where plates overlap in a complex way
and hundreds of tremors are observed every year.
On March 11, 2011, a massive earthquake struck the Tohoku region.
At magnitude 9.
0, it was one of the largest ever recorded.
The tsunami that followed swept the coasts of Iwate, Miyagi, and Fukushima
taking the lives of about twenty thousand people.
At the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear plant
the earthquake and tsunami caused the loss of cooling function
and a serious accident — a meltdown — occurred.
Hundreds of thousands of people were forced into evacuation from the land they had long called home.
Right after the disaster, first responder such as firefighters, police officers
and Self - Defense Force members continued to rescue people from the rubble.
Much aid was also sent from around the world.
Preparedness for disaster has now become common sense in Japanese society.
Each household stocks emergency food and water, and schools and workplaces hold evacuation drill regularly.
The early warning system of the meteorological agency is one of the most precise in the world.
Over ten years have passed since the Great East Japan Earthquake
and the affected area has been walking the road of reconstruction step by step.
But not every wound has healed.
We carry the responsibility of passing on the lesson learned from the disaster to the next generation.
